blocks:
Ayahs

أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْفِيلِ (١)
Have you not regarded how your Lord dealt with the army of the elephants? (1)
أَلَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِى تَضْلِيلٍ (٢)
Did He not make their stratagems go awry, (2)
وَأَرْسَلَ عَلَيْهِمْ طَيْرًا أَبَابِيلَ (٣)
and send against them flocks of birds (3)
تَرْمِيهِم بِحِجَارَةٍ مِّن سِجِّيلٍ (٤)
pelting them with stones of shale, (4)
فَجَعَلَهُمْ كَعَصْفٍ مَّأْكُولٍۭ (٥)
thus making them like chewed-up straw? (5)

Content and Virtue of Surah "Al-Fil"

This Surah, as its name suggests, refers to a famous historical story that took place in the year of the birth of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, where Allah protected the House of "Kaaba" from the evil of a massive army of disbelievers who had come from the land of Yemen riding elephants.

This Surah recalls that wondrous story which many people of Mecca remembered, as it had occurred in the recent past.

Recalling this story serves as a warning to the arrogant and stubborn disbelievers, so they may know that they possess not the slightest power against the power of Allah. The God who crushed the great army of elephants with those small birds and those semi-hardened pebbles (حجارة من سجيل) is capable of punishing these stubborn, arrogant ones as well.

Neither was their power greater than the power of "Abrahah," nor did their army and numbers ever reach that extent; meaning, you who witnessed this event with your own eyes, why do you not descend from the steed of arrogance?! Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام states: "Whoever recites Surah Al-Fil in his obligatory prayers, on the Day of Resurrection, every mountain, level plain, and clod of earth will testify for him that he was among the prayer-performers. A caller will then cry out: 'You have spoken the truth regarding My servant; I accept your testimony for him or against him. Admit My servant into Paradise without reckoning, for he is one whom I love and whose deeds I love.'" 1 It is obvious that all this virtue, reward, and great recompense belong to the one who, by reading these verses, descends from the steed of arrogance and walks the path of Divine pleasure.

1: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 539.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

أَ لَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحابِ الْفِيلِ (1) أَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِي تَضْلِيلٍ (2) وَ أَرْسَلَ عَلَيْهِمْ طَيْراً أَبابِيلَ (3) تَرْمِيهِمْ بِحِجارَةٍ مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ (4)

فَجَعَلَهُمْ كَعَصْفٍ مَأْكُولٍ (5)

Occasion of Revelation

In a Hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام, we read: "Abu Talib used to defend the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله with his sword until he says: (One day) Abu Talib said: 'O son of my brother! Have you been sent to all people, or only to your own tribe?'

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: 'No, I have been sent to all human beings, from the white and the black, the Arab and the non-Arab. By the One in Whose hand is my soul, I invite all white and black humans to this religion, and I call all those upon the peaks of mountains and across the seas to this religion, and I invite all the languages of Persia and Rome.'

When this reached the ears of the Quraysh, they were astonished and said: 'Do you not hear what your brother's son is saying? By Allah, if the people of Persia and Rome hear these words, they will snatch us from our land! And they will tear the stones of the Kaaba apart piece by piece!' It was then that Allah revealed the noble verse: وَ قالُوا إِنْ نَتَّبِعِ الْهُدى‌ مَعَكَ نُتَخَطَّفْ مِنْ أَرْضِنا أَ وَ لَمْ نُمَكِّنْ لَهُمْ حَرَماً آمِناً يُجْبى‌ إِلَيْهِ ثَمَراتُ كُلِّ شَيْ‌ءٍ: 'They say: If we follow the guidance with you, we shall be snatched away from our land. Have We not established for them a secure sanctuary to which are brought the fruits of all things?' (Al-Qasas: 57).

And regarding their statement that they would demolish the Kaaba, He revealed Surah Al-Fil" (reminding them that no one is capable of such a thing). 1

1: "Rawdat al-Wa'izin" (as narrated in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 669, Hadith 8).

The Story of the Companions of the Elephant

Commentators and historians have narrated this story in various ways, and they debate the year of its occurrence. However, the essence of the story is so famous that it is considered among the mutawatir (successively narrated) reports. We present it here based on the well-known narrations summarized from "Sirat Ibn Hisham," "Bulugh al-Arab," "Bihar al-Anwar," and "Majma' al-Bayan":

The king "Dhu Nuwas" subjected the Christians of Najran, who lived near that land, to severe torture to force them to renounce Christianity (the Quran mentions this event as the "Companions of the Pit" in Surah Al-Buruj, and we explained it in detail in the commentary of that Surah).

After this great crime, a man named "Daws" from among them escaped and reached the "Caesar of Rome," who was of the Christian faith, and explained the situation to him.

Since the distance between Rome and Yemen was great, Caesar wrote a letter to "Najashi" (the Negus), the King of Abyssinia, asking him to take revenge for the Christians of Najran from Dhu Nuwas, and sent the letter with that same person to Najashi.

Najashi dispatched a massive army of seventy thousand men under the command of a person named "Aryat" to Yemen. "Abrahah" was also one of the commanders of this army.

Dhu Nuwas was defeated, and Aryat became the ruler of Yemen. After some time, Abrahah revolted against him, eliminated him, and took his place.

News of this reached Najashi, and he decided to suppress Abrahah. To save himself, Abrahah shaved his head and sent it along with some soil from Yemen to Najashi as a sign of complete submission and declared his loyalty.

When Najashi saw this, he forgave him and retained him in his post.

At this time, to prove his service, Abrahah built a very beautiful and significant cathedral, the likes of which did not exist on earth at that time. Subsequently, he decided to call the people of the Arabian Peninsula to it instead of the "Kaaba," intending to make it the center of the Arab pilgrimage and transfer the great centrality of Mecca to that location.

For this purpose, he sent many missionaries to the surrounding areas, among the Arab tribes and the land of Hijaz. The Arabs, who were deeply attached to Mecca and the Kaaba and considered it among the great legacies of Ibrahim the Friend of Allah, felt threatened.

According to some narrations, a group came and secretly set the cathedral on fire, and according to another report, some defiled it secretly. In this way, they showed a strong reaction against this great invitation and discredited Abrahah's temple.

Abrahah became furious and decided to completely demolish the House of Kaaba, both to take revenge and to turn the Arabs toward the new temple. He set out for Mecca with a massive army, some of whose riders used elephants.

When he approached Mecca, he sent men to plunder the camels and property of the people of Mecca, and in the process, two hundred camels belonging to "Abdul Muttalib" were seized.

Abrahah sent someone into Mecca and told him to find the leader of Mecca and tell him: "Abrahah, the King of Yemen, says: I have not come for war; I have only come to demolish this House of Kaaba. If you do not resort to war, I have no need to shed your blood!" Abrahah's messenger entered Mecca and searched for the chief and noble of Mecca. Everyone pointed him to Abdul Muttalib. He recounted the matter to Abdul Muttalib, who replied: "We do not have the power to fight you, and as for the House of Kaaba, Allah Himself will protect it."

The messenger told Abdul Muttalib: "You must come with me to him." When Abdul Muttalib entered Abrahah's presence, the king was deeply impressed by Abdul Muttalib's tall stature, attractive appearance, and extraordinary majesty. So much so that Abrahah rose from his place out of respect and sat on the ground, seating Abdul Muttalib beside him, as he did not want to seat him on the throne next to himself. Then he told his translator: "Ask him what his need is."

The translator said: "My need is that two hundred camels have been plundered from me; order that my property be returned."

Abrahah was greatly surprised by this request and said to his translator: "Tell him: When I saw you, a greatness of you settled in my heart, but with these words you have spoken, you have become small in my eyes. You speak of your two hundred camels, yet you say absolutely nothing about the Kaaba, which is your religion and the religion of your ancestors, and which I have come to demolish?!" Abdul Muttalib replied: "I am the lord of the camels, and this House has a Lord Who will protect it!" (These words shook Abrahah and left him in deep thought).

Abdul Muttalib returned to Mecca and informed the people to take refuge in the surrounding mountains. He himself, with a group, came to the side of the Kaaba to pray and seek help. He took hold of the ring of the Kaaba's door and recited his famous verses:

"O Allah! Every man protects his home, so protect Your homes!" "Never let their cross and their power ever overcome Your power!" "They have dragged all their lands and the elephant to take Your family captive!" "O Allah! Every man protects his family, so protect Your family!" "And help Your people today against the people of the cross and its worshippers!" 1

Then Abdul Muttalib went to one of the valleys around Mecca and took refuge there with a group of the Quraysh, and he ordered one of his sons to go to the top of Mount Abu Qubays to see what was happening.

His son quickly returned to his father and said: "Father! A black cloud is visible from the direction of the sea (the Red Sea) coming toward our land." Abdul Muttalib was delighted and cried out: "O assembly of Quraysh! Enter your homes, for Allah has brought you victory from Himself." This was on one hand.

On the other hand, Abrahah, riding his famous elephant named "Mahmud," descended toward Mecca from the surrounding mountains with his vast army to crush the Kaaba. But no matter how much he pressured his elephant, it would not move forward. However, when they turned its head toward Yemen, it would move rapidly. Abrahah was greatly astonished by this and fell into bewilderment.

At that moment, birds arrived from the direction of the sea, like swallows, and each of them carried three pebbles: one in its beak and two in its claws, roughly the size of a chickpea. They dropped these pebbles upon Abrahah's army, and whoever was struck by them perished. Some have said that the pebbles would pierce through whatever part of the body they fell upon and exit from the other side.

At this moment, a strange terror overshadowed Abrahah's entire army. Those who remained alive fled, asking the way to Yemen to return, but they continuously fell to the ground like autumn leaves along the road.

Abrahah himself was also struck by a stone and wounded. They took him back to Sana'a (the capital of Yemen), where he passed away.

Some have said that the first time the diseases of measles and smallpox were seen in the land of the Arabs was that year.

Regarding the number of elephants Abrahah brought, some have written it was only the elephant "Mahmud," some say eight, some ten, and some twelve.

In this same year, according to the famous report, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was born, and the world was illuminated by the light of his existence. Therefore, a group believes there was a connection between these two events.

In any case, the importance of this great incident was such that they named that year "Am al-Fil" (the Year of the Elephant), and it became the starting point of the Arab calendar. 1

1: Historians and commentators have recorded the above verses differently; what is presented above is a summary combined from various narrations. 1: "Sirat Ibn Hisham," Vol. 1, pp. 38-62; "Bulugh al-Arab," Vol. 1, pp. 250-263; "Bihar al-Anwar," Vol. 15, p. 130 onwards; "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 542.

Commentary

Tell Abrahah Not to Come in Haste!

In the first verse of this Surah, addressing the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, He says:

"Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the companions of the elephant?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحابِ الْفِيلِ‌).

They had come with all that army and power to demolish the House of God, and Allah crushed them with an army that was seemingly very small and insignificant. He disabled the elephants with small birds and neutralized the advanced weapons of that day with "Sijjil" pebbles, to make manifest and clear the weakness and helplessness of this arrogant and headstrong human before Divine power.

The expression "Have you not seen?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ), despite the fact that this event occurred before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was born or coincided with his birth, is because the said event was very close to the era of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Furthermore, it was so famous, well-known, and widely reported (mutawatir) that it was as if the Prophet had witnessed it with his own blessed eyes, and many of the Prophet's contemporaries had certainly seen it with their own eyes.

The term "Companions of the Elephant" is due to those few elephants they had brought with them from Yemen to intimidate the opponents and cause the camels and horses to bolt upon seeing them and flee the battlefield. 1

Then He adds: "Did He not make their plan end in frustration and ruin?" (أَ لَمْ يَجْعَلْ كَيْدَهُمْ فِي تضليلٍ‌).

They intended to destroy the House of Kaaba, hoping to give centrality to the cathedral in Yemen and turn all the Arab tribes toward it. However, not only did they fail to reach their goal, but this event—the fame of which spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula—increased the majesty of Mecca and the House of Kaaba, turned the hearts of the longing ones toward it more than ever before, and granted that land greater security.

The meaning of "Tadlil," which is to lead astray or cause to fail, is that they never achieved their objective.

Then, proceeding to the details of this event, He says: "And He sent against them birds in flocks" (وَ أَرْسَلَ عَلَيْهِمْ طَيْراً أَبابِيلَ‌).

"Ababil," contrary to what is common in popular speech, was not the name of that bird; rather, it has a descriptive meaning. Some have considered it to mean "separate groups," in the sense that the said birds came "group after group" from every side toward the army of the elephant.

1: Although "Fil" (elephant) is singular here, it carries a generic and collective meaning.

This word has a collective meaning; some consider its singular to be "Ababilah," meaning a group of birds, horses, or camels, while others say it is a plural that has no singular of its own kind.

In any case, "Tayr" here has a collective meaning, and these two words "Tayr" and "Ababil" together mean "birds in flocks" (not that Ababil is the name of those birds).

As for what kind of bird this was, as we mentioned in the narration of the story, the famous view is that they were birds similar to swallows or swifts that rose from the direction of the Red Sea and came after the army of the elephant.

In the next verse, He adds: "Striking them with stones of Sijjil (petrified clay)" (تَرْمِيهِمْ بِحِجارَةٍ مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ‌). 1 As we narrated in the account of this event from histories, commentaries, and traditions, each of these small birds carried three pebbles the size of a chickpea or smaller, carrying one in its beak and two in its feet. These small stones, upon hitting anyone, would cause them to perish!

As He says in the next verse: "And He made them like eaten straw!" (فَجَعَلَهُمْ كَعَصْفٍ مَأْكُولٍ‌).

"Asf" refers to the leaves on the stalks of crops that have dried and been crushed; in other words, it means "straw." Some have interpreted it as the husk of wheat while it is still in the ear.

The first meaning is more appropriate here.

The expression "Ma'kul" (eaten) indicates that this straw has been crushed once more under the teeth of an animal, completely disintegrated, and then the animal's stomach has crushed it for a third time. This shows that the pebbles completely disintegrated whoever they fell upon! This expression, besides proving the intensity of their disintegration, is an allusion to the worthlessness, weakness, and helplessness of this rebellious, arrogant, and seemingly powerful group and assembly.

1: "Sijjil" is a Persian word derived from "Sang" (stone) and "Gil" (clay); thus, it is something that is neither as hard as stone nor as soft as clay.

Key Points

1. A Peerless Miracle! (This House has a Lord!)

It is interesting that the Holy Quran has presented this detailed and long story in a few short and striking sentences, with the utmost eloquence and rhetoric. In fact, it has pointed to aspects that serve the goals of the Quran, namely awakening the arrogant tyrants and showing human weakness before the great power of Allah.

This event shows that miracles and extraordinary feats—contrary to what some have imagined—do not necessarily have to appear at the hands of a Prophet or an Imam; rather, they occur in any circumstances Allah wills and deems necessary. The goal is for people to become acquainted with the greatness of Allah and the truth of His religion.

This wondrous and miraculous punishment has a clear difference from the punishments of other rebellious nations. For punishments such as the Flood of Nuh, the earthquake and stoning of the people of Lut, the gale of the people of 'Ad, and the lightning of the people of Thamud were a series of natural events whose occurrence in those specific conditions was the miracle.

However, the story of the destruction of Abrahah's army by pebbles falling from the beaks and feet of those small birds is not something that resembles natural events.

The rising of those small birds, their coming toward that specific army, their carrying of pebbles, their specific aiming, and the disintegration of the bodies of a massive army by those small stones are all extraordinary matters. Yet, we know these are very insignificant before the power of Allah.

The God Who created atomic power within these very pebbles—which, if released, would produce a massive explosion—finds it easy to create a property in them that would make the bodies of Abrahah's army like "Asf Ma'kul" (crushed and eaten straw).

There is no need, as some Egyptian commentators have done, to justify this event by saying the stones carried germs of cholera, typhoid, or smallpox. 1 If it is mentioned in some narrations that blood and pus came from the bodies of the victims like those afflicted with smallpox, it is not proof that they were necessarily afflicted with smallpox.

Similarly, there is no need to say that these pebbles were compressed atoms whose internal voids had vanished, making them extraordinarily heavy such that they pierced through wherever they fell.

1: Refer to "Tafsir Abduh," Juz' Amma, p. 158.

These are all justifications mentioned to make this event appear natural, and we see no need for them. We know enough to say that these stones possessed such a wondrous property that they disintegrated bodies; we have no further information beyond this. In any case, no task is difficult before the power of Allah. 1

2. The Severest Punishment with the Smallest Means!

It is noteworthy that Allah demonstrated His power against the arrogant and the tyrants in this event in the most sublime manner. Perhaps a punishment more severe than that of Abrahah's army cannot be found in the world—that a group should be so crushed as to become like crushed and eaten straw (Asf Ma'kul).

To destroy a population with all that power and glory, weak pebbles and small, frail birds like swallows were used. This is a warning to all the tyrants and arrogant ones of the world, so they may know to what extent they are helpless before His power! Sometimes Allah even entrusts these great missions to even smaller creatures; for example, He commissions a microbe that can never be seen with the eye to multiply rapidly in a short time and afflict powerful nations with a dangerous contagious disease like "cholera" or "plague," causing them all to fall to the ground like autumn leaves in a short period.

The great "Ma'rib" dam in Yemen—as we said in the commentary of Surah Saba—was the means of creating abundant prosperity and a great, powerful civilization. Subsequently, the rebellion of this people increased, but the command for its destruction—as mentioned in some narrations—was entrusted to one or a few field mice! They were to penetrate that great dam and create a hole in it. Due to the seepage of water, this hole gradually became larger and larger; finally, the great dam collapsed, and the water accumulated behind it destroyed all those settlements, houses, and palaces, and that great population was destroyed or scattered and wandered in other regions. This is the manifestation of the power of the Great God.

3. The Objectives of the Story of "Al-Fil"

From the next Surah (Surah Quraysh), it is clearly understood that one of the objectives of Surah Al-Fil is to recall the memory of the great and massive blessings of Allah upon the Quraysh, to show them that if it were not for the grace of the Lord, neither would there be any trace of this sacred center—Mecca and the Kaaba—nor of the Quraysh. Perhaps they might descend from the steed of pride and arrogance and submit to the call of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

On the other hand, this event, which coincided with the blessed birth of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, was in truth a preparation for that great manifestation and a herald of the majesty of this uprising. This is what commentators refer to as "Irhas." 1 Thirdly, it is a threat to all the tyrants of the world, whether from the Quraysh or others, that they may know they can never stand against the power of the Lord. It is better for them to cast away vain thoughts and submit to His command, yielding to truth and justice.

Fourthly, it shows the importance of this great House; when the enemies of the "Kaaba" plotted its destruction and wanted to transfer the centrality of this Abrahamic land elsewhere, Allah gave them such a lesson that it became a source of warning for all and increased the importance of this sacred center.

Fifthly, the God Who answered the prayer of Ibrahim the Friend regarding the security of this sacred land and guaranteed it, showed in this event that His Will has decreed that this center of monotheism and worship shall always be a secure sanctuary.

4. A Certain Historical Event

It is noteworthy that the event of the "Companions of the Elephant" was so certain among the Arabs that it became the starting point of their history. As we said, the Holy Quran mentions it with the beautiful expression "Have you not seen?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ), addressing the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله who was not present at that time and did not see it, which is another sign of the certainty of this event.

Beyond this, when the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited these verses to the polytheists of Mecca, not a single person denied it. Had it been a doubtful matter, at least a group would have objected, and their objection would have been recorded in history like their other objections, especially since the Quran expressed the matter with the phrase "Have you not seen?"

Furthermore, the majesty of this sacred House is established by this certain historical miracle.

1: If it is mentioned in some histories that the first time measles and smallpox were seen in Arab lands was in that year, it does not serve as proof for this meaning.

O Allah! Grant us the success to guard this great center of monotheism. O Lord! Cut off the hands of those who intend to suffice only with preserving the outward appearances of this sacred center while ignoring its message of truth.

O God! Grant the pilgrimage of it, with complete awareness and gnosis, to all the longing ones.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Fil.
